elements.sent-2411-001: Mathematical diffraction theory has been developed since about 1995.
elements.sent-2411-002: Hof's initial approach relied on tempered distributions in euclidean space.
elements.sent-2411-003: Nowadays often the Fourier theory by Argabright and Gil de Lamadrid is used, which applies to appropriate measures on locally compact abelian groups.
elements.sent-2411-004: We review diffraction theory using Wiener amalgams as test function spaces.
elements.sent-2411-005: For translation bounded measures, this unifies and simplifies the former two approaches.
elements.sent-2411-006: We treat weighted versions of Meyer's model sets as examples.
elements.sent-2411-007: Mathematical diffraction theory has emerged from the discovery of quasicrystals, in order to describe their unusual diffraction properties.
elements.sent-2411-008: From a mathematical point of view, it is concerned with Fourier analysis of translation bounded measures, which serve as an idealisation of a piece of matter.
elements.sent-2411-009: Hof's influential approach \cite{FRSS-Hof1 used tempered distributions in euclidean space.}
elements.sent-2411-010: An earlier approach by de Bruijn relied on Gelfand-Shilov test functions, a certain subclass of Schwartz functions \cite{FRSS-B86,FRSS-L00 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: But yet another framework is prominently being used, namely the Fourier theory of measures by Argabright and Gil de Lamadrid, called AG theory in the sequel.
elements.sent-2411-012: It extends the Fourier theory of positive definite measures based on Bochner's theorem \cite{FRSS-BF75 and has been developed in the 1970ies \cite{FRSS-ARMA1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The AG theory proved useful for diffraction analysis for three reasons.
elements.sent-2411-014: Firstly, any translation bounded measure indeed admits an autocorrelation measure and a diffraction measure as its Fourier transform~\cite{FRSS-BaakeLenz2004 , while the corresponding problem for tempered distributions is open \cite{FRSS-ST16 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Secondly, the AG theory had already incorporated almost periodicity \cite{FRSS-AG79, FRSS-ARMA .}
elements.sent-2411-016: As almost periodicity had been recognised central for diffraction analysis \cite{FRSS-L00 , the AG theory could readily be used to study the nature of the diffraction based on the autocorrelation measure \cite{FRSS-BaakeMoody2004, FRSS-MoSt, FRSS-S19 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Moreover, it recently appeared that important classes of pure point diffractive translation bounded measures are characterised by various types of almost periodicity \cite{FRSS-LSS2020a,FRSS-LLRSS, FRSS-LRS2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Thirdly, the AG theory applies to measures on locally compact abelian groups beyond euclidean space, in a simpler way than Schwartz--Bruhat tempered distributions.
elements.sent-2411-019: Such generality was indeed needed, as models for quasicrystals had emerged that were constructed from non-euclidean internal spaces \cite{FRSS-BaakeMoody2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: These can be analysed using the Poisson summation formula of the underlying non-euclidean lattice~\cite{FRSS-CRS .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However using this framework seemed subtle, as the Fourier transform is not a bijection on the space of AG transformable measures.
elements.sent-2411-022: This holds as the Fourier transform of a measure is required to be a measure in the AG theory, which constrains the transform to be translation bounded~\cite[Thm.~2.5]{FRSS-ARMA1 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Whereas the question of twice AG transformability was posed as an open problem \cite[p.~50]{FRSS-ARMA1 , a simple answer was given already in 1979: A measure is twice AG transformable if and only if it is AG transformable and translation bounded \cite{FRSS-Fei79a, FRSS-Fei80 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: This explains why AG transformable translation bounded measures constitute a natural space for diffraction analysis.
elements.sent-2411-025: As the above characterisation remained neglected in diffraction analysis until recently \cite{FRSS-RiSchu20 , it is the aim of this article to provide sufficient background.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The above characterisation has been obtained within the framework of Wiener amalgam spaces \cite{FRSS-F83, FRSS-Fei81c .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Two important ingredients, which we will review below, are the identification of translation bounded measures as the bounded linear functionals on the Wiener algebra \cite{FRSS-LRW74 , and Feichtinger's mild distributions \cite{FRSS-Fei81, FRSS-Jak, FRSS-Fei23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Mild distributions can be defined on locally compact abelian groups, where they constitute a well tractable substantial subclass of Schwartz--Bruhat tempered distributions.
elements.sent-2411-029: As any AG transformable measure is a mild distribution, the AG theory is subsumed by mild distribution theory.
elements.sent-2411-030: We will thus review the AG theory using mild distributions, thereby providing detailed proofs of statements in \cite{FRSS-Fei79a, FRSS-Fei80 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: In particular, we will give a characterisation of AG transformability that is considerably simpler than the original definition, and we will prove the above characterisation of twice AG transformability.
elements.sent-2411-032: We will also rephrase diffraction theory of translation bounded measures using mild distributions.
elements.sent-2411-033: While this streamlines arguments in comparison to the traditional approaches \cite{FRSS-Hof1, FRSS-BaakeLenz2004 , this also indicates extensions beyond translation bounded measures.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We will finally review weighted versions of Meyer's model sets using mild distributions.
elements.sent-2411-035: This will rely on a Poisson summation formula for test functions including Feichtinger's algebra, which extends \cite{FRSS-M19, FRSS-M22 beyond euclidean space.}
elements.sent-2411-036: We thus obtain diffraction results that improve on previous work, as summarised in \cite{FRSS-CRS .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Altogether, we demonstrate that mild distributions provide a flexible instrument for diffraction analysis, which subsumes and simplifies the traditional approaches on translation bounded measures.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our article is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-039: We start with a detailed discussion of bounded uniform partitions of unity in Sections~\ref{FRSS-sec:bupu and \ref{FRSS-cbupu .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: We then carefully review the Wiener algebra in Section~\ref{FRSS-sec:WA and Feichtinger's algebra in Section~\ref{FRSS-sect:appB , using bounded uniform partitions of unity.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The space dual to Feichtinger's algebra, the mild distributions, is discussed in Section~\ref{FRSS-sec:fmd .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Section~\ref{FRSS-sec:m reviews translation bounded measures and mild measures.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The central Section \ref{FRSS-sec:FTm reviews AG transformability using mild distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Section~\ref{FRSS-sec:dtr reviews diffraction theory from the mild distribution viewpoint, taking into account recent developments \cite{FRSS-LSS2020a on pure point diffraction.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Section~\ref{FRSS-sec:ppdpsr applies mild distribution theory to weighted model sets.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The main result of that section has been announced elsewhere \cite{FRSS-R23 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Throughout this article, $G$ denotes a locally compact abelian (LCA) group, and $\widehat G$ denotes its dual LCA group.
elements.sent-2411-048: We choose Haar measures $m_G$ on $G$ and $m_{\widehat G}$ on $\widehat G$ such that Plancherel's formula holds.
elements.sent-2411-049: We denote by $\COsp(G)$ the algebra of continuous functions on $G$ vanishing at infinity ($f\in \COsp(G)$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists compact $K\subseteq G$ such that $|f|\le\varepsilon$ on $G\setminus K$), by $\Cu(G)\subseteq \COsp(G)$ the algebra of uniformly continuous and bounded functions, and by $\Cc(G)\subseteq \Cu(G)$ the algebra of continuous functions on $G$ having compact support.
elements.sent-2411-050: For an LCA group $G$, its Wiener algebra $\Wsp(G)$ and Feichtinger's algebra $\So(G)$ are subalgebras of $\COsp(G)$ that consist of functions with prescribed local and global behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-051: This can conveniently be formulated using bounded uniform partitions of unity\footnote{Our discussion may be complemented by the recent survey \cite{FRSS-Fei24 .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Recall that a partition of unity\index{partition~of~unity $(\phi_i)_{i\in I}$ on $G$ is a collection of functions $\phi_i\in \Cc(G)$ such that $\sum_{i\in I \phi_i=1$, where the sum is finite at each $x \in G$.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: In order to motivate the notion of bounded uniform partition of unity, consider a tiling of $G=\RR^d$ by translates of non-empty compact subsets $P_1, P_2, \ldots , P_k$ of $\RR^d$, which are called the prototiles.
elements.sent-2411-054: Thus for each $j$ there exists a discrete set $\vL_j\subset \RR^d$ such that \begin{equation}\RR^d= \bigcup_{j=1}^k (\Lambda_{j}+P_j)=\bigcup_{j=1}^k\bigcup_{t \in \Lambda_j} (t+P_j) \ ,\end{equation} where all tiles $t+P_j$ in the above union have mutually disjoint interior, compare \cite[Def.~5.2]{FRSS-TAO1 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: If all prototiles have a boundary of zero Lebesgue measure, then \begin{equation}\sum_{j=1}^k \sum_{t \in \vL_j} 1_{t+P_j}(x) =1\end{equation} for Lebesgue-almost all $x\in\RR^d$, where $1_A$ is the characteristic function of the set $A$.
elements.sent-2411-056: We would like to have an equality which holds everywhere, where the characteristic functions of tiles are replaced by continuous functions.
elements.sent-2411-057: This can be achieved by a partition of unity.
elements.sent-2411-058: But exactly as in the tiling case, we would like to additionally use uniformly bounded functions of uniformly bounded support.
elements.sent-2411-059: The following simple example gives a bounded uniform partition of unity on $G=\RR$ based on a tiling of the real line by an interval prototile.
elements.sent-2411-060: Then $(\phi_n)_{n\in \ZZ}$ is a partition of unity of $\RR$, where each function is bounded by $1$ and has support of length $2$.

elements.sent-2411-064: Then $(\phi_{n,\alpha )_{n\in \ZZ}$ is a partition of unity of $\RR$, where each function is bounded by $1$ and has support of length $2\alpha$.}

elements.sent-2411-075: For a bounded uniform partition of unity $(\phi_i)_{i\in I}$, we need to describe where the functions $\phi_i$ are located.
elements.sent-2411-076: We use indexed families of points in $G$ for that purpose.
elements.sent-2411-077: Let us stress here that we do not use point sets, as we want to allow for finitely many repetitions of points.
elements.sent-2411-078: Indeed, finitely many repetitions occur naturally when refining a given bounded uniform partition of unity, see Remark~\ref{FRSS-rem:refbupu .}
elements.sent-2411-079: For an indexed point family $X=(x_i)_{i\in I}$, we call the set $\supp(X)=\{x_i: i\in I\}$ the supporting set of $X$\index{supporting~set .}
elements.sent-2411-080: We then have $\supp(X)=\supp(\delta_X)$ for the support of the point measure $\delta_X=\sum_{i\in I}\delta_{x_i}\index{Dirac~comb!weighted~Dirac~comb}$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Using the multiplicity function $r_X: G\to \NN_0\cup \{\infty\}$, defined by $r_X(x)=\card\{i\in I: x_i=x\}$, we can write $\delta_X=\sum_{x\in \supp(\delta_X) r_X(x)\delta_x$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Thus $\delta_X$ is a weighted Dirac comb.
elements.sent-2411-083: In order to describe properties of indexed point families, we extend some standard notions for sets \cite[Ch.~2.1]{FRSS-TAO1 .}
elements.sent-2411-084: A point family $X$ is called uniformly discrete\index{uniformly~discrete if the set $\supp(X)$ is uniformly discrete, i.e., there exists an open zero neighborhood $U\subseteq G$ such that $(x+U)\cap(y+U)=\varnothing$ holds for all distinct $x,y\in \supp(X)$, compare \cite[p.~278]{FRSS-St17 .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: In that case we will also refer to $X$ as being a $U$-uniformly discrete point family.
elements.sent-2411-086: A point family $X$ is called relatively dense\index{relatively~dense if the set $\supp(X)$ is relatively dense, i.e., there exists a compact set $K\subseteq G$ such that $\supp(X)+K=G$.}
elements.sent-2411-087: We will sometimes call $X$ a $K$-relatively dense point family in that case.
elements.sent-2411-088: A point family $X$ is called Delone\index{Delone if $\supp(X)$ is a Delone set, i.e., if $\supp(X)$ is both uniformly discrete and relatively dense.}
elements.sent-2411-089: A point family $X$ is called weakly uniformly discrete\index{weakly~uniformly~discrete if its multiplicity function $r_X$ is bounded and if the set $\supp(X)$ is weakly uniformly discrete, i.e., $\supp(X)$ is a finite union of uniformly discrete sets, compare \cite[p.~278]{FRSS-St17 .}}
elements.sent-2411-090: It can be shown \cite[Thm.~14]{FRSS-fe92 that weak uniform discreteness of $X$ is equivalent to translation boundedness of the associated weighted Dirac comb $\delta_{X}$.}
